title: Sharding a Vector Index
concept: Splitting a large vector collection across multiple machines and merging results at query time.

exercises.prompt: In LLM application engineering, what is a vector database primarily used for?

options.text: Finding pieces of data with similar semantic meaning using embeddings
Strictly storing traditional SQL tables using keys and indexes
Compressing large language model weights into smaller ZIP files

exercises.explain: Vector databases store high-dimensional embeddings generated by models, allowing us to find text or media with similar semantic meanings, not just matching keywords.

cards.title: The Scale Bottleneck
cards.body: As your LLM app grows, you might end up with millions of high-dimensional vectors. A single server will eventually run out of RAM and search too slowly. How do we break this massive pile of data into manageable pieces?

cards.title: What is Sharding?
cards.body: Sharding is the process of splitting a massive vector index into smaller, self-contained pieces called shards. Each shard is stored and searched on a separate computer, allowing you to scale out as your data grows.

cards.title: The Multi-Branch Library
cards.body: Imagine a library with millions of books. Instead of stuffing every book into a single massive room, the library builds three separate branches. To find the best sci-fi book, you ask librarians at all three branches simultaneously and compile their top recommendations.

cards.title: Parallel Search in Action
cards.body: If you have 10 million vector embeddings from your company documents, you can split them into 5 shards of 2 million vectors each. When a user asks a question, all 5 shards search their 2 million vectors in parallel, combining the results instantly.

cards.title: The 'Smart Routing' Illusion
cards.body: You might think sharding requires a complex central computer to know exactly where similar vectors live. In reality, vectors are usually distributed randomly or round-robin across shards, meaning every shard must be queried to guarantee we find the absolute closest matches.

cards.title: Single Server vs. Sharded Index
cards.body: Comparing a single giant server versus a sharded vector database cluster.

left.text: Holds all 10M vectors. Search is limited by one machine's RAM and CPU speed.

right.text: Vectors split across 3 nodes. Search runs in parallel, utilizing triple the CPU power.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary speed benefit of sharding a vector index?

options.text: It decreases the total number of dimensions in each vector.
It allows searching multiple smaller indexes in parallel across different machines.
It guarantees that only one machine ever needs to be turned on.
It converts dense vectors into simple text files to save space.

exercises.explain: Because we split the database across multiple machines, we can search all shards at the same time (in parallel) instead of waiting to scan one giant index sequentially.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To find the true nearest neighbors in a standard sharded database, the query must typically be sent to all shards.
exercises.explain: Because similar vectors could be located on any shard, the query must be sent to all shards to find the true overall nearest neighbors.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing component in a sharded vector search architecture:
exercises.sentence: The coordinator gathers local results from every shard and merges them.
exercises.explain: A coordinator node is responsible for receiving the user's query, broadcasting it to all individual shards, and merging the sorted results.

exercises.prompt: Identify the response that accurately describes a technical challenge of querying sharded vector indexes.

options.text: Sharding is when you write SQL queries using JOIN statements to combine table rows based on primary keys.

When querying sharded vector indices, the system uses a scatter-gather pattern. This introduces network latency because the coordinator must wait for the slowest shard to return its local nearest neighbors before merging.
To shard a vector, you divide its dimensions by the number of cluster nodes. For example, a 1536-dimensional vector becomes 512 dimensions on 3 nodes.

exercises.explain: Option B shows an AI assistant explaining the trade-offs of scatter-gather querying (high network overhead and latency from waiting for the slowest node) during vector database sharding. Option A is a generic database explanation, and Option C is an irrelevant math calculation.

exercises.prompt: When a user queries a sharded vector database to find the top 5 nearest neighbors, how does the system retrieve the correct results?

options.text: Each node holds a complete copy of the index and answers queries independently without talking to other nodes
The entire database is searched on one master node, while other nodes store backup text files
The query is broadcast to all shards, each returns its local top results, and a coordinator merges them
Vectors are sorted by size, and only the shard containing the exact mathematical average is searched

exercises.explain: Sharding splits the vector data across nodes, so each node only searches its local slice. When a query comes in, it must be sent to all shards. A coordinator node then collects the top results from each shard and merges them to find the true global top matches.
